title: Associations between respiratory diseases and lung cancer risk: a secondary analysis of the large-scale prospective UK biobank cohort
abstract: AbstractIntroductionRespiratory diseases are significant risk factors for lung cancer; however, the association between acute respiratory infections and lung cancer incidence requires further exploration.MethodsWe performed a secondary analysis of the prospective UK Biobank cohort, including participants aged 37–73 years recruited from 22 assessment centres across the UK between 2006 and 2010. Cox proportional hazards models estimated HRs for incident lung cancer according to respiratory disease status, which was defined based on linked hospital inpatient records. Mediation analysis explored potential biomarkers, and Mendelian randomisation assessed causal relationships.ResultsDuring a mean follow-up of 10.44 years (4 790 738 person-years), 2189 participants developed lung cancer. Among 107 007 individuals with respiratory diseases, 1322 cases occurred (incidence rate 27.6 per 10 000 person-years), compared with 867 cases among 351 876 participants without respiratory diseases (7.9 per 10 000 person-years). Overall, respiratory diseases were associated with increased lung cancer risk (HR 2.97, 95% CI 2.75 to 3.21). Acute respiratory infections, including acute nasopharyngitis (HR 3.41; 95% CI 1.83 to 6.34), influenza (HR 3.90; 95% CI 2.53 to 6.01), viral pneumonia (HR 9.86; 95% CI 6.55 to 14.85) and bacterial pneumonia (HR 6.28; 95% CI 4.40 to 8.96), showed strong associations with lung cancer incidence. In subtype analyses, squamous cell carcinoma exhibited the highest risk elevation (HR 3.65; 95% CI 3.06 to 4.36). Mediation analysis indicated that neutrophil counts partially mediated these associations (proportion mediated up to 8%).ConclusionAcute respiratory infections were associated with higher lung cancer incidence, providing hypothesis-generating evidence that may inform future risk stratification research.
discussion: DiscussionIn this exploratory analysis of a large prospective cohort from the UK Biobank, we observed that respiratory diseases were associated with an increased incidence of lung cancer. Previous epidemiological studies have found that many chronic respiratory diseases increase the risk of lung cancer incidence, and our study confirms this finding.31152 Compared with previous studies, our work adds a more comprehensive description of these associations across a broad spectrum of respiratory conditions, including acute infections, within a single population-based dataset. As expected, HRs were smaller after grouping diagnoses by the first qualifying respiratory condition, which better accounts for coexisting diseases and increases sample size, yielding more stable estimates. These findings may help to deepen our understanding of the epidemiology of respiratory diseases and lung cancer, but they should be interpreted as hypothesis-generating rather than definitive evidence of causality. Importantly, they do not justify a change in clinical practice or screening protocols; instead, they provide a rationale for evaluating these associations in future research focused on refined risk evaluation. In subgroup analysis, respiratory diseases were associated with multiple lung cancer subtypes (NSCLC, SCLC, LUAD and LUSC), with the strongest elevation observed for lung squamous cell carcinoma.Regarding the underlying mechanisms of this phenomenon, chronic respiratory diseases may increase lung cancer risk through a sustained proinflammatory microenvironment.1214 This environment generates oxidative stress that damages DNA or induces apoptosis, leading to the accumulation of mutations and an elevated cancer risk.151753 Socioeconomic deprivation may further heighten lung cancer risk through higher smoking rates and greater exposure to urban air pollution. Recent evidence shows that PM2.5 can promote carcinogenesis by enhancing inflammatory expansion of pre-existing mutant clones, providing additional environmental context to the findings.54For acute respiratory infections, the epidemiological literature remains limited and sometimes conflicting. To our knowledge, associations of acute nasopharyngitis and acute pharyngitis with subsequent lung cancer have not been well characterised in prior population studies and therefore require replication. For pneumonia, prior reports have described higher short-term detection and/or higher incidence of lung cancer after pneumonia, while other studies have suggested null or inverse associations.2124 In this study, we observed that several acute respiratory infections—including acute nasopharyngitis, acute pharyngitis, influenza and pneumonia phenotypes—were associated with higher lung cancer incidence, but this observed association is hypothesis-generating and needs replication with longer follow-up and clearer temporal separation.55Biologically, acute infections may transiently disrupt the airway epithelial barrier and impair tissue repair, potentially increasing vulnerability to inhaled carcinogens.5557 Findings from COVID-19 provide relevant context: SARS-CoV-2 infection can cause substantial epithelial/endothelial injury and prolonged inflammatory perturbations, which could, in theory, contribute to tissue vulnerability after recovery. At the same time, the COVID-19 era also plausibly increased the detection of subclinical lung cancers due to higher rates of chest imaging during and after acute illness, raising the possibility of detection bias for acute infections and pleural conditions.175861 Mechanistically, acute infection-triggered inflammation has been proposed to involve cytokine signalling (eg, tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-α) and downstream pathways such as Janus kinase (JAK)1/2–signal transducer and activator of transcription (STAT)1, among others, which could reshape the pulmonary microenvironment and influence epithelial survival and repair. However, whether these pathways mediate the causal effect of acute infections on lung cancer initiation or progression remains uncertain and requires further experimental and epidemiological investigation.6264We also observed increased lung cancer risk associated with several pleural and related conditions (eg, pulmonary oedema, empyema and pleural plaques). Prior evidence is sparse or mixed for some of these phenotypes;65 66 therefore, these results should be interpreted cautiously and prioritised for external validation, ideally with careful control for imaging intensity and underlying cardiopulmonary comorbidity.In subgroup analyses, men with respiratory diseases had a higher relative risk of lung cancer than women. This difference may reflect sex-patterned distributions of smoking and other exposures across respiratory conditions rather than a sex-specific biological effect.6770 We also observed higher lung cancer risk among participants with respiratory diseases who reported more frequent baseline alcohol intake. The literature on alcohol and lung cancer is heterogeneous, and in our study, alcohol intake was measured only at baseline;7173 thus, this association may capture correlated lifestyle patterns rather than a direct causal effect. Given the borderline p value (0.046), the alcohol-related finding should be interpreted as exploratory.Mediation analyses suggested that leucocyte- and neutrophil-related measures may partially account for some associations between respiratory diseases and lung cancer, although the PM was modest. These markers should be viewed as exploratory signals rather than validated mediators or clinical biomarkers.74 In contrast, MR analyses did not support a causal relationship for most respiratory phenotypes. Considering that we applied multiple strategies to reduce reverse causation and confounding in the observational analyses (including excluding lung cancers diagnosed within 1 year after respiratory disease diagnosis, restricting the analytic cohort, multivariable adjustment and sensitivity analyses), nonetheless, residual confounding remains plausible. Consequently, the discrepancy between observational and MR findings may indicate that respiratory diseases function as markers of underlying exposures (eg, smoking, alcohol use, adiposity or other shared determinants) that contribute to inflammation and lung cancer risk, rather than being direct causes themselves.Because this study is observational and key lifestyle behaviours (including smoking and alcohol use) were assessed only at baseline, the findings regarding lifestyle factors are descriptive and hypothesis-generating rather than prescriptive. Although we observed differences in lung cancer incidence across behaviour categories among participants with respiratory diseases, these associations do not establish causality and do not provide a basis for specific lifestyle or screening recommendations beyond existing public health guidance. Future studies should focus on whether these respiratory associations can improve the precision of current risk-based screening models.A key strength of this study is the use of a large-scale prospective cohort, enabling evaluation of incidence risks across a broad range of respiratory diseases and lung cancer subtypes. We accounted for multiple covariates, reduced potential reverse causation by excluding lung cancers diagnosed soon after respiratory disease onset and used complementary approaches (mediation and MR) to explore potential biomarkers and causal inference.Nonetheless, this study has limitations. First, rare respiratory diseases were excluded due to limited case numbers. Second, respiratory disease ascertainment relied strictly on hospital inpatient records, meaning the findings primarily reflect severe episodes. Consequently, milder infections managed in primary care or outpatient settings were not captured, potentially underestimating the total association burden. Third, our grouping strategy focused on the earliest qualifying respiratory diagnosis and did not fully model coexisting respiratory conditions over time. Fourth, covariates were measured at a single time point and may have changed during follow-up. Fifth, despite Bonferroni correction, chance findings cannot be fully excluded; for some acute upper respiratory infections (URIs), sparse subsequent lung cancer events may yield statistical instability. Importantly, detection and work-up bias remain plausible in this design. Acute infections and pleural effusions often trigger diagnostic imaging, which may lead to the incidental detection of subclinical lung cancers. Moreover, the nature of UK-based coding data makes it difficult to definitively verify the accuracy of infection codes or distinguish whether observed associations reflect true causality, coding errors or coinfections. This potential for detection bias should be considered when interpreting the strong associations observed for these acute conditions. Furthermore, our MR analyses should be regarded as exploratory: stringent instrument selection and F-statistic thresholds meant that only a small number of SNPs (with modest variance explained) were available for several phenotypes, which may produce imprecise or unstable estimates; larger GWAS with expanded sample sizes will be required for more definitive causal inference. Finally, the over-representation of Whites in the UK Biobank cohort may limit generalisability to more ethnically diverse populations,75 76 and registry-based linkage data may contain some diagnostic misclassification despite systematic quality assurance.In conclusion, in this secondary analysis of the UK Biobank, respiratory diseases—including certain acute lower respiratory infections—were associated with an increased subsequent incidence of lung cancer. These findings reflect observed associations and should be interpreted with caution, considering the inherent risk of residual confounding and detection bias in observational cohorts. Mediation analyses suggested that inflammation-related blood markers (notably neutrophils) explained only a modest proportion of these associations. Further studies in independent cohorts and experimental settings are needed to confirm these observations and clarify the underlying mechanisms.77 78
figure_legends: Study participants and study design. SNPs, single nucleotide polymorphisms.

Respiratory diseases associated with lung cancer. ‘People with diseases’ and ‘People without diseases’ represent lung cancer cases/person-years in the exposed and unexposed groups, respectively. Model 1 is a univariate Cox analysis. Model 2 is adjusted for age and sex. Model 3 is adjusted for age, BMI, Townsend Deprivation Index, waist-to-hip ratio, sex, ethnicity, family history of cancer, education level, smoking status, alcohol consumption frequency, oily fish intake and processed meat intake.

Subgroup analysis of respiratory diseases associated with lung cancer. The Cox proportional hazards model was adjusted for age, body mass index (BMI), Townsend Deprivation Index, waist-to-hip ratio (WHR), sex, ethnicity, family history of cancer, education level, smoking status, alcohol consumption frequency, oily fish intake and processed meat intake.

Proportion mediated (PM) of mediating biomarkers between respiratory diseases and lung cancer. The Cox proportional hazards model was adjusted for age, body mass index (BMI), Townsend Deprivation Index, waist-to-hip ratio, sex, ethnicity, family history of cancer, education level, smoking status, alcohol consumption frequency, intake of oily fish and intake of processed meat.
